eatAutoCode Welcome 0.1 Package Functions 0.2 Data Expectations

eatAutoCode IQB Welcome eatAutoCode provides R wrapper functions for the IQB auto-coder API. It combines response data from IQB Testcenter with coding schemes from IQB Studio and returns tabular coding results that can be inspected, joined, or exported in an R workflow. The package is useful when you want to: code one response object for a unit, code many response objects in one call, add or overwrite codes with manual scoring information, inspect variable dependencies in a coding scheme, or extract variable locations from unit definitions. The book is organized around the regular workflow: install the package, prepare coding schemes and response data, run the coder, then inspect the output. 0.1 Package Functions The exported functions are: code_responses() for coding one unit response object. code_responses_array() for coding several unit response objects at once. get_dependency_tree() for inspecting how coding variables depend on each other. extract_variable_location() for extracting variable locations from unit definitions. 0.2 Data Expectations Most functions accept list objects parsed from JSON or JSON strings directly. In practice, the input usually comes from: IQB Studio coding schemes, IQB Testcenter response data, optional manual coding data, and unit definitions when variable locations should be extracted. Where possible, keep the raw JSON exports unchanged until they are parsed or passed into eatAutoCode. That makes the workflow easier to audit and repeat.
Chapter 1 Installation 1.1 Development Version 1.2 Local Development Checkout 1.3 Rendering This Book

Chapter 1 Installation 1.1 Development Version Install the development version from GitHub: install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("iqb-research/eatAutoCode") Then load the package: library(eatAutoCode) 1.2 Local Development Checkout If you are working from a local clone of the repository, install the package dependencies and load the package with devtools: install.packages("devtools") devtools::load_all(".") The package wraps JavaScript code shipped in inst/index.js. If the JavaScript dependencies are updated, rebuild the bundled auto-coder before testing the package: packer::npm_install() packer::npm_install("@iqb/responses") After updating the JavaScript bundle, run the tests: devtools::test() 1.3 Rendering This Book The documentation is a bookdown project that renders into the docs/ directory: bookdown::render_book("index.Rmd") The docs/ directory can be used as the source folder for GitHub Pages.

Chapter 2 Workflows 2.1 Coding One Response Object 2.2 Coding With Manual Overrides 2.3 Coding Multiple Response Objects 2.4 Handling List Values and Missing Values 2.5 Inspecting Dependencies 2.6 Extracting Variable Locations

Chapter 2 Workflows 2.1 Coding One Response Object Use code_responses() when you have one coding scheme and one response object for a unit. library(eatAutoCode) coding_scheme <- jsonlite::read_json("coding-scheme.json") responses <- jsonlite::read_json("responses.json") coded <- code_responses( coding_scheme = coding_scheme, responses = responses ) coded The result is a data frame with one row per coded variable. The usual columns are: id: variable identifier from the coding scheme, status: coding status, value: response value used for coding, key: matching key, when available, code: assigned code, and score: assigned score. 2.2 Coding With Manual Overrides Manual coding data can be supplied to add or overwrite codes. This is useful when individual responses have already been scored by hand or need to be forced to a specific code. manual_codes <- list( list(id = "b1", status = "CODING_COMPLETE", code = 31, score = 1), list(id = "b2", status = "CODING_COMPLETE", code = -99, score = 0) ) coded <- code_responses( coding_scheme = coding_scheme, responses = responses, manual = manual_codes ) 2.3 Coding Multiple Response Objects Use code_responses_array() when the same coding scheme should be applied to multiple response objects. response_array <- list( list(unit_key = "A1", responses = responses), list(unit_key = "A2", responses = responses), list(unit_key = "A3", responses = responses) ) coded_array <- code_responses_array( coding_scheme = coding_scheme, responses = response_array ) coded_array The returned data frame includes unit_key, so coded rows can be traced back to the original response object. 2.4 Handling List Values and Missing Values code_responses_array() and extract_variable_location() coerce list-like values to character values. By default, list values are wrapped with [[[ and ]]] and collapsed with ;;;. coded_array <- code_responses_array( coding_scheme = coding_scheme, responses = response_array, collapse = ";;;", wrap_start = "[[[", wrap_end = "]]]", missing = "___MISSING___" ) The missing placeholder is transformed to NA in the returned data frame. 2.5 Inspecting Dependencies Coding schemes can contain variables that depend on other variables. Use get_dependency_tree() to inspect this structure before coding or debugging a coding scheme. dependency_tree <- get_dependency_tree(coding_scheme) dependency_tree The result includes the variable id, its dependency level, its sources, and the page. 2.6 Extracting Variable Locations Use extract_variable_location() with unit definitions that contain a definition column. Because this operation is memory-consuming, process small batches of unit definitions. units <- tibble::tibble( unit_key = c("unit_01", "unit_02"), definition = c(unit_definition_json_01, unit_definition_json_02) ) locations <- extract_variable_location(units) locations For large exports, split the units into batches before calling the function.
Chapter 3 Function Reference 3.1 code_responses() 3.2 code_responses_array() 3.3 get_dependency_tree() 3.4 extract_variable_location()

Chapter 3 Function Reference 3.1 code_responses() code_responses(coding_scheme, responses, manual = "null") Codes one response object with one coding scheme. Arguments: coding_scheme: list or character string containing a coding scheme from IQB Studio Lite. responses: list or character string containing unit responses from IQB Testcenter. manual: optional list or character string with manual codes. The default "null" is treated like missing manual data. Returns a data frame. 3.2 code_responses_array() code_responses_array( coding_scheme, responses, collapse = ";;;", wrap_start = "[[[", wrap_end = "]]]", missing = "___MISSING___" ) Codes several response objects with one coding scheme. Arguments: coding_scheme: list or character string containing a coding scheme from IQB Studio Lite. responses: list or character string containing an array of response objects. collapse: separator for concatenating list values. wrap_start: prefix used when wrapping list values. wrap_end: suffix used when wrapping list values. missing: placeholder that is converted to NA. Returns a data frame. The value column is coerced to character, so list entries can be represented in a rectangular table. 3.3 get_dependency_tree() get_dependency_tree(coding_scheme) Displays the variable dependency tree of a coding scheme. Arguments: coding_scheme: list or character string containing a coding scheme from IQB Studio Lite. Returns a data frame with dependency information. 3.4 extract_variable_location() extract_variable_location( units, collapse = ";;;", wrap_start = "[[[", wrap_end = "]]]", missing = "___MISSING___" ) Extracts variable locations from unit definitions. Arguments: units: tibble with a definition column containing unit definitions. collapse: separator for concatenating list values. wrap_start: prefix used when wrapping list values. wrap_end: suffix used when wrapping list values. missing: placeholder that is converted to NA. Returns a data frame.
Chapter 4 Development 4.1 Repository Layout 4.2 Updating Documentation 4.3 Testing 4.4 Publishing With GitHub Pages

Chapter 4 Development 4.1 Repository Layout Important paths in the repository: R/: R wrapper functions exported by the package. srcjs/: TypeScript and JavaScript source code for the auto-coder bridge. inst/index.js: bundled JavaScript shipped with the R package. tests/testthat/: package tests and test fixtures. man/: generated R documentation. docs/: rendered bookdown site. 4.2 Updating Documentation Function documentation is written in roxygen comments in the R/ files. After editing those comments, regenerate the manual pages and namespace: devtools::document() The bookdown pages are written in the top-level .Rmd files listed in _bookdown.yml. Render the book after editing pages: bookdown::render_book("index.Rmd") 4.3 Testing Run the package tests after changes to the R wrappers or JavaScript bundle: devtools::test() The tests include examples for: coding one response object, coding arrays of response objects, applying manual coding data, and inspecting dependency trees. 4.4 Publishing With GitHub Pages This book is configured to render into docs/. In the GitHub repository settings, set Pages to publish from the docs/ folder on the main branch.
